The **St. Lawrence River** ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Fleuve Saint-Laurent*, pronounced [[flœv sɛ̃ lɔʁɑ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French")) is a large international [river](/wiki/River "River") in the middle [latitudes](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") of [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") connecting the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") with the [North](/wiki/North "North") [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"). Its waters flow in a northeasterly direction from [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario") to the [Gulf of St. Lawrence](/wiki/Gulf_of_St._Lawrence "Gulf of St. Lawrence"), traversing [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") and [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") in Canada and [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") in the United States. A portion of the river demarcates the [Canada–United States border](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_border "Canada–United States border").

As the primary [drainage outflow](/wiki/Discharge_(hydrology) "Discharge (hydrology)") of the [Great Lakes Basin](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Basin "Great Lakes Basin"), the St. Lawrence has the [second-highest discharge](/wiki/List_of_rivers_by_discharge "List of rivers by discharge") of any [river](/wiki/River "River") in [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") (after the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River")) and the 16th-highest in the world. The [estuary of the St. Lawrence](/wiki/Estuary_of_St._Lawrence "Estuary of St. Lawrence") is often cited by scientists as the largest in the world. Significant natural landmarks of the river and estuary include the 1,864 river islands of the [Thousand Islands](/wiki/Thousand_Islands "Thousand Islands"), the endangered whales of [Saguenay–St. Lawrence Marine Park](/wiki/Saguenay%E2%80%93St._Lawrence_Marine_Park "Saguenay–St. Lawrence Marine Park"), and the limestone monoliths of the [Mingan Archipelago](/wiki/Mingan_Archipelago_National_Park_Reserve "Mingan Archipelago National Park Reserve").

Long a transportation route to [Indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada"), the St. Lawrence River has played a key role in the [history of Canada](/wiki/History_of_Canada "History of Canada") and in the development of cities such as [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") and [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City"). The river remains an important shipping route as the backbone of the [St. Lawrence Seaway](/wiki/St._Lawrence_Seaway "St. Lawrence Seaway"), a lock and canal system that enables world marine traffic to access the inland ports of the [Great Lakes Waterway](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Waterway "Great Lakes Waterway").

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Lawrence_River&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The river has historically been given a variety of different names by local [First Nations](/wiki/First_Nations_in_Canada "First Nations in Canada"). Beginning in the 16th century, French explorers visited what is now Canada and gave the river names such as the *Grand fleuve de [Hochelaga](/wiki/Hochelaga_(village) "Hochelaga (village)")* and the *Grande rivière du Canada*,[[8]](#cite_note-topo-8) where *fleuve* and *rivière* are two [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") words (*fleuve* being a river that flows into the sea).

The river's present name has been used since 1604 when it was recorded on a map by [Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain").[[8]](#cite_note-topo-8) Champlain opted for the names *Grande riviere de sainct Laurens* and *Fleuve sainct Laurens* in his writings, supplanting the earlier names.[[8]](#cite_note-topo-8) In contemporary French, the name is rendered as the *fleuve Saint-Laurent*. The name *Saint-Laurent* (Saint Lawrence) was originally applied to the [eponymous bay](/wiki/Gulf_of_Saint_Lawrence "Gulf of Saint Lawrence") by [Jacques Cartier](/wiki/Jacques_Cartier "Jacques Cartier") upon his arrival into the region on the 10th of August [feast day](/wiki/Calendar_of_saints "Calendar of saints") for [Saint Lawrence](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence "Saint Lawrence") in 1535.[[8]](#cite_note-topo-8)

[Indigenous people](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_in_Canada "Indigenous peoples in Canada") use the following names:

* [Innu-aimun](/wiki/Innu-aimun "Innu-aimun"), the language of [Nitassinan](/wiki/Nitassinan "Nitassinan"), refer to it as *Wepistukujaw Sipo*/*Wepìstùkwiyaht sīpu*.[[8]](#cite_note-topo-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Lozier-9)
* the [Abenaki](/wiki/Abenaki "Abenaki") call it *Moliantegok*/*Moliantekw* ("Montréal River"),[[8]](#cite_note-topo-8) *Kchitegw*/*Ktsitekw*/*Gicitegw* ("Great River"),[[9]](#cite_note-Lozier-9) or *Oss8genaizibo*/*Ws8genaisibo*/*Wsogenaisibo* ("River of the Algonquins").[[9]](#cite_note-Lozier-9)
* in [Mohawk language](/wiki/Mohawk_language "Mohawk language") it is *Roiatatokenti*, *Raoteniateara*,[[8]](#cite_note-topo-8) *Ken’tarókwen*,[[10]](#cite_note-10) or *Kaniatarowanénhne*.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
* [Tuscarora people](/wiki/Tuscarora_people "Tuscarora people") call it *Kahnawáˀkye* or *Kaniatarowanenneh* ("Big Water Current").[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* [Algonquin people](/wiki/Algonquin_people "Algonquin people") call it "the Walking Path" or *Magtogoek*,[[8]](#cite_note-topo-8)[[13]](#cite_note-13) or *Kitcikanii sipi*, the "Large Water River".[[9]](#cite_note-Lozier-9)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* the [Wendat Nation](/wiki/Wendat_Nation "Wendat Nation") call it *Lada8anna* or *Laooendaooena'*.[[9]](#cite_note-Lozier-9)
* the [Atikamekw](/wiki/Atikamekw "Atikamekw") of [Nitaskinan](/wiki/Nitaskinan "Nitaskinan") refer to it as *[Micta sipi](https://atj.wikipedia.org/wiki/Micta_sipi "atj:Micta sipi")* ("Huge River").

### Marine weather

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Lawrence_River&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Marine weather")]

In winter, the St. Lawrence River begins producing ice in December between [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") and [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City"). The prevailing winds and currents push this ice towards the estuary,[[15]](#cite_note-Estuary_and_Gulf_of_St._Lawrence_-_Fisheries_and_Oceans_Canada-15) and it reaches the east of [Les Méchins](/wiki/Les_M%C3%A9chins "Les Méchins") at the end of December. Ice covers the entire [Gulf of St. Lawrence](/wiki/Gulf_of_St._Lawrence "Gulf of St. Lawrence") in January and February.

Ice helps navigation by preventing the formation of waves, and therefore spray, and prevents the icing of ships.[[16]](#cite_note-The_secrets_of_the_Saint-Laurent,_marine_weather_guide-16)

### Watershed

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Lawrence_River&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Watershed")]

With the draining of the [Champlain Sea](/wiki/Champlain_Sea "Champlain Sea"), due to a rebounding continent from the [Last Glacial Maximum](/wiki/Last_Glacial_Maximum "Last Glacial Maximum"), the St. Lawrence River was formed. The Champlain Sea lasted from about 13,000 years ago to about 10,000 years ago and was continuously shrinking during that time, a process that continues today.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Miller2015-18) The head of the St. Lawrence River, near [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario"), is home to the [Thousand Islands](/wiki/Thousand_Islands "Thousand Islands").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Today, the St. Lawrence River begins at the outflow of Lake Ontario and flows adjacent to [Cape Vincent](/wiki/Cape_Vincent_(village),_New_York "Cape Vincent (village), New York"), [Gananoque](/wiki/Gananoque,_Ontario "Gananoque, Ontario"), [Clayton](/wiki/Clayton,_New_York "Clayton, New York"), [Alexandria Bay](/wiki/Alexandria_Bay,_New_York "Alexandria Bay, New York"), [Brockville](/wiki/Brockville "Brockville"), [Morristown](/wiki/Morristown,_New_York "Morristown, New York"), [Ogdensburg](/wiki/Ogdensburg,_New_York "Ogdensburg, New York"), [Massena](/wiki/Massena_(village),_New_York "Massena (village), New York"), [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall,_Ontario "Cornwall, Ontario"), [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), [Trois-Rivières](/wiki/Trois-Rivi%C3%A8res "Trois-Rivières"), and [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City") before draining into the [Gulf of St. Lawrence](/wiki/Gulf_of_St._Lawrence "Gulf of St. Lawrence"), often given as the largest [estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary") in the world.[[20]](#cite_note-20) The estuary begins at the eastern tip of [Île d'Orléans](/wiki/%C3%8Ele_d%27Orl%C3%A9ans "Île d'Orléans"), just downstream from Quebec City.[[7]](#cite_note-BenkeCushing2005-7) The river becomes [tidal](/wiki/Tide "Tide") around Quebec City.[[21]](#cite_note-Dawson2007-21)

The St. Lawrence River runs 3,058 [kilometres](/wiki/Kilometre "Kilometre") (1,900 [miles](/wiki/Mile "Mile")) from the farthest headwater to the mouth and 1,197 kilometres (743.78 mi) from the outflow of Lake Ontario. These numbers include the estuary; without the estuary, the length from Lake Ontario is 500 kilometres (311 mi). The farthest headwater is the [North River](/wiki/North_River_(Minnesota) "North River (Minnesota)") in the [Mesabi Range](/wiki/Mesabi_Range "Mesabi Range") at [Hibbing](/wiki/Hibbing "Hibbing"), Minnesota. Its drainage area, which includes the Great Lakes, the world's largest system of freshwater lakes, is 1,344,200 [square kilometres](/wiki/Square_kilometre "Square kilometre") (518,998.5 [square miles](/wiki/Square_mile "Square mile")), of which 839,200 km2 (324,016.9 sq mi) is in Canada and 194,981.6 square miles (505,000 km2) is in the United States. The basin covers parts of [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") and [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") in Canada, parts of [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"), [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana"), [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"), and nearly the entirety of the state of [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan") in the United States. The average discharge below the [Saguenay River](/wiki/Saguenay_River "Saguenay River") is 16,800 cubic metres per second (590,000 cu ft/s). At Quebec City, it is 12,101 m3/s (427,300 cu ft/s). The average discharge at the river's source, the outflow of Lake Ontario, is 7,410 m3/s (262,000 cu ft/s).[[7]](#cite_note-BenkeCushing2005-7)

The St. Lawrence River includes [Lake Saint Francis](/wiki/Lake_Saint_Francis_(Canada) "Lake Saint Francis (Canada)") at [Salaberry-de-Valleyfield](/wiki/Salaberry-de-Valleyfield "Salaberry-de-Valleyfield"), [Lake Saint-Louis](/wiki/Lake_Saint-Louis "Lake Saint-Louis") south of Montreal and [Lake Saint Pierre](/wiki/Lake_Saint_Pierre "Lake Saint Pierre") east of Montreal. It encompasses four [archipelagoes](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago"): the [Thousand Islands](/wiki/Thousand_Islands "Thousand Islands") chain near [Alexandria Bay, New York](/wiki/Alexandria_Bay,_New_York "Alexandria Bay, New York") and [Kingston, Ontario](/wiki/Kingston,_Ontario "Kingston, Ontario"); the [Hochelaga Archipelago](/wiki/Hochelaga_Archipelago "Hochelaga Archipelago"), including the [Island of Montreal](/wiki/Island_of_Montreal "Island of Montreal") and [Île Jésus](/wiki/%C3%8Ele_J%C3%A9sus "Île Jésus") ([Laval](/wiki/Laval,_Quebec "Laval, Quebec")); the Lake St. Pierre Archipelago (classified a biosphere world reserve by the UNESCO in 2000)[[22]](#cite_note-22) and the smaller [Mingan Archipelago](/wiki/Mingan_Archipelago "Mingan Archipelago"). Other islands include Île d'Orléans near Quebec City and [Anticosti Island](/wiki/Anticosti_Island "Anticosti Island") north of the [Gaspé](/wiki/Gasp%C3%A9_Peninsula "Gaspé Peninsula"). It is the second-longest river in Canada.

[Lake Champlain](/wiki/Lake_Champlain "Lake Champlain") and the [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa_River "Ottawa River"), [Richelieu](/wiki/Richelieu_River "Richelieu River"), [Saint-Maurice](/wiki/Saint-Maurice_River "Saint-Maurice River"), [Saint-François](/wiki/Saint-Francois_River "Saint-Francois River"), [Chaudière](/wiki/Chaudi%C3%A8re_River "Chaudière River") and Saguenay rivers drain into the St. Lawrence.

The St. Lawrence River is in a seismically active zone where [fault](/wiki/Fault_(geology) "Fault (geology)") reactivation is believed to occur along late [Proterozoic](/wiki/Proterozoic "Proterozoic") to early [Paleozoic](/wiki/Paleozoic "Paleozoic") normal faults related to the opening of the [Iapetus Ocean](/wiki/Iapetus_Ocean "Iapetus Ocean"). The faults in the area are [rift](/wiki/Rift "Rift")-related and comprise the [Saint Lawrence rift system](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_rift_system "Saint Lawrence rift system").

According to the United States Geological Survey, the St. Lawrence Valley is a [physiographic province](/wiki/Physiographic_regions_of_the_world "Physiographic regions of the world") of the larger [Appalachian](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains") division, containing the [Champlain](/wiki/Champlain_Valley "Champlain Valley") section.[[23]](#cite_note-USGS-Water-23) However, in Canada, where most of the valley is, it is instead considered part of a distinct [St. Lawrence Lowlands](/wiki/St._Lawrence_Lowlands "St. Lawrence Lowlands") physiographic division, and not part of the Appalachian division.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Sources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Lawrence_River&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Sources")]

The source of the North River in the [Mesabi Range](/wiki/Mesabi_Range "Mesabi Range") in [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") (Seven Beaver Lake) is considered to be the source of the St. Lawrence River. Because it crosses so many lakes, the water system frequently changes its name. From source to mouth, the names are:

* [North River](/wiki/North_River_(Minnesota) "North River (Minnesota)")[[25]](#cite_note-Britannica-25)
* [Saint Louis River](/wiki/Saint_Louis_River_(Lake_Superior_tributary) "Saint Louis River (Lake Superior tributary)")[[26]](#cite_note-Encarta-26)
* [Lake Superior](/wiki/Lake_Superior "Lake Superior")
* [St. Marys River](/wiki/St._Marys_River_(Michigan-Ontario) "St. Marys River (Michigan-Ontario)")
* [Lake Huron](/wiki/Lake_Huron "Lake Huron")
* [St. Clair River](/wiki/St._Clair_River "St. Clair River")
* [Lake St. Clair](/wiki/Lake_St._Clair "Lake St. Clair")
* [Detroit River](/wiki/Detroit_River "Detroit River")
* [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie")
* [Niagara River](/wiki/Niagara_River "Niagara River")
* [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario")

The St. Lawrence River also passes through [Lake Saint-Louis](/wiki/Lake_Saint-Louis "Lake Saint-Louis") and [Lake Saint-Pierre](/wiki/Lake_Saint-Pierre "Lake Saint-Pierre") in Quebec.

## Biodiversity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Lawrence_River&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Biodiversity")]

The diversity of the St. Lawrence River includes:[[27]](#cite_note-The_St._Lawrence,_this_great_river_that_flows_within_us_(David_Suzuki_Foundation)-27)

* 19 species of [marine mammals](/wiki/Marine_mammal "Marine mammal")
* More than 230 species of [birds](/wiki/Bird "Bird")
* Nearly 35 species of [amphibians](/wiki/Amphibian "Amphibian") and [reptiles](/wiki/Reptile "Reptile")
* 200 species of freshwater and saltwater fish (including 19 [sharks](/wiki/Shark "Shark") and rays)
* 2200 [invertebrates](/wiki/Invertebrate "Invertebrate") in the [estuary](/wiki/Estuary_of_St._Lawrence "Estuary of St. Lawrence") and its [gulf](/wiki/Gulf_of_St._Lawrence "Gulf of St. Lawrence") ([sponges](/wiki/Sponge "Sponge"), [jellyfish](/wiki/Jellyfish "Jellyfish"), [corals](/wiki/Coral "Coral"), [crustaceans](/wiki/Crustacean "Crustacean"), etc.)
* Nearly 2000 [vascular plants](/wiki/Vascular_plants "Vascular plants")[[28]](#cite_note-Flore_laurentienne-28)

### First Nations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Lawrence_River&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: First Nations")]

Flowing through and adjacent to numerous [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_Canada "Indigenous peoples of Canada") homelands, the river was a primary thoroughfare for many peoples. Beginning in [Dawnland](/wiki/Dawnland "Dawnland") at the Gulf of St. Lawrence, the river borders [Mi'kma'ki](/wiki/Mi%27kma%27ki "Mi'kma'ki")[[37]](#cite_note-Gaspe-37) in the South (what is today known as the [Canadian Maritimes](/wiki/Canadian_Maritimes "Canadian Maritimes")), and [Nitassinan](/wiki/Nitassinan "Nitassinan") in the North, the national territory of the [Innu people](/wiki/Innu_people "Innu people").[[38]](#cite_note-38) On the south shore beyond the [Mi'kmaw](/wiki/Mi%27kmaq_people "Mi'kmaq people") district of [Gespe'gewa'ki](/wiki/Gasp%C3%A9_Peninsula "Gaspé Peninsula"),[[37]](#cite_note-Gaspe-37) the river passes Wolastokuk (the [Maliseet](/wiki/Maliseet "Maliseet") homeland), Pαnawαhpskewahki (the [Penobscot](/wiki/Penobscot "Penobscot") homeland), and Ndakinna (the [Abenaki](/wiki/Abenaki "Abenaki") homeland).[[39]](#cite_note-native-land-39) Continuing, the river passes through the former country of the [St. Lawrence Iroquois](/wiki/St._Lawrence_Iroquoians "St. Lawrence Iroquoians") and then three of the six homelands of the [Haudenosaunee](/wiki/Haudenosaunee "Haudenosaunee"): the [Mohawk or Kanienʼkehá꞉ka](/wiki/Mohawk_people "Mohawk people"), the [Oneida or Onyota'a:ka](/wiki/Oneida_people "Oneida people"), and the [Onondaga or Onöñda’gaga’](/wiki/Onondaga_people "Onondaga people").[[39]](#cite_note-native-land-39)

In the early 17th century, the [Wendat Nation](/wiki/Wendat_Nation "Wendat Nation") migrated from their original country of [Huronia](/wiki/Huronia_(region) "Huronia (region)") to what is now known as [Nionwentsïo](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nionwents%C3%AFo "fr:Nionwentsïo") centred around [Wendake](/wiki/Wendake,_Quebec "Wendake, Quebec").[[40]](#cite_note-Nionwentsio-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) Nionwentsïo occupies both the north and south shores of the river,[[40]](#cite_note-Nionwentsio-40) overlapping with Nitassinan and the more western [Wabanaki](/wiki/Wabanaki_Confederacy "Wabanaki Confederacy") or Dawnland countries.[[39]](#cite_note-native-land-39) Adjacent on the north shore is the Atikamekw territorial homeland of [Nitaskinan](/wiki/Nitaskinan "Nitaskinan")[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) and, upstream, the further reaches of Anishinaabewaki, specifically the homelands of the [Algonquin](/wiki/Algonquin_people "Algonquin people") and [Mississauga](/wiki/Mississaugas "Mississaugas") Nations.[[39]](#cite_note-native-land-39)

### European exploration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Lawrence_River&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: European exploration")]

The [Norse explored](/wiki/Norse_colonization_of_the_Americas "Norse colonization of the Americas") the Gulf of St. Lawrence in the 11th century and were followed by fifteenth- and early sixteenth-century European mariners, such as [John Cabot](/wiki/John_Cabot "John Cabot"), and the brothers [Gaspar](/wiki/Gaspar_Corte_Real "Gaspar Corte Real") and [Miguel Corte-Real](/wiki/Miguel_Corte-Real "Miguel Corte-Real"). The first European explorer known to have sailed up the St. Lawrence River itself was [Jacques Cartier](/wiki/Jacques_Cartier "Jacques Cartier"). At that time, the land along the river described as "about two leagues, a mountain as tall as a heap of wheat" was inhabited by the [St. Lawrence Iroquoians](/wiki/St._Lawrence_Iroquoians "St. Lawrence Iroquoians"). During Cartier's second voyage in 1535, because Cartier arrived in the estuary on [Saint Lawrence](/wiki/Lawrence_of_Rome "Lawrence of Rome")'s feast day 10 August, he named it the *Gulf of Saint Lawrence*.[[44]](#cite_note-44)
[[45]](#cite_note-45)

The St. Lawrence River is today partly within the U.S. and as such is that country's sixth oldest surviving European place-name.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Early colonists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Lawrence_River&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Early colonists")]

The earliest regular Europeans in the area were the [Basques](/wiki/History_of_the_Basque_people#Modern_Period "History of the Basque people"), who came to the St Lawrence Gulf and River in pursuit of whales from the early 16th century. The [Basque whalers](/wiki/History_of_Basque_whaling "History of Basque whaling") and fishermen traded with indigenous Americans and set up settlements, leaving vestiges all over the coast of eastern Canada and deep into the St. Lawrence River. Basque commercial and fishing activity reached its peak before the *[Armada Invencible](/wiki/Armada_Invencible "Armada Invencible")'*s disaster (1588), when the Basque whaling fleet was confiscated by King [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain"). Initially, the whaling galleons from [Labourd](/wiki/Labourd "Labourd") were not affected by the Spanish defeat.

Until the early 17th century, the French used the name *Rivière du Canada* to designate the St. Lawrence upstream to Montreal and the Ottawa River after Montreal. The St. Lawrence River served as the main route for European exploration of the North American interior, first pioneered by French explorer [Samuel de Champlain](/wiki/Samuel_de_Champlain "Samuel de Champlain").

### Colonial control

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Lawrence_River&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Colonial control")]

Control of the river was crucial to [British strategy to capture New France](/wiki/Great_Britain_in_the_Seven_Years_War "Great Britain in the Seven Years War") in the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"). Having [captured Louisbourg](/wiki/Siege_of_Louisbourg_(1758) "Siege of Louisbourg (1758)") in 1758, the British sailed up to Quebec the following year thanks to charts drawn up by [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook"). British troops were ferried via the St. Lawrence to attack the city from the west, which they successfully did at the [Battle of the Plains of Abraham](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Plains_of_Abraham "Battle of the Plains of Abraham"). The river was used again by the British [to defeat](/wiki/Battle_of_Neuville "Battle of Neuville") the French siege of Quebec under the [Chevalier de Lévis](/wiki/Chevalier_de_L%C3%A9vis "Chevalier de Lévis") in 1760.

In 1809, the first steamboat to ply its trade on the St. Lawrence was built and operated by [John Molson](/wiki/John_Molson "John Molson") and associates, a scant two years after Fulton's steam-powered navigation of the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"). The *[Accommodation](/wiki/PS_Accommodation "PS Accommodation")* with ten passengers made her maiden voyage from Montreal to Quebec City in 66 hours, for 30 of which she was at anchor. She had a keel of 75 feet, and a length overall of 85 feet. The cost of a ticket was eight dollars upstream, and nine dollars down. She had berths that year for twenty passengers.[[47]](#cite_note-St_Lawrence_Saga:_The_Clarke_Steamship_Story_-_Before_Clarke_Steamship-47)
Within a decade, daily service was available in the hotly-contested Montreal-Quebec route.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Because of the virtually impassable [Lachine Rapids](/wiki/Lachine_Rapids "Lachine Rapids"), the St. Lawrence was once continuously navigable only as far as Montreal. Opened in 1825, the [Lachine Canal](/wiki/Lachine_Canal "Lachine Canal") was the first to allow ships to pass the rapids. An extensive system of canals and locks, known as the [St. Lawrence Seaway](/wiki/St._Lawrence_Seaway "St. Lawrence Seaway"), was officially opened on 26 June 1959 by [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") (representing Canada) and President [Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower") (representing the United States). The Seaway (including the [Welland Canal](/wiki/Welland_Canal "Welland Canal")) now permits ocean-going vessels to pass all the way to [Lake Superior](/wiki/Lake_Superior "Lake Superior").[[49]](#cite_note-The_St._Lawrence_Seaway,_a_Vital_Waterway-49)

### Modern Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Lawrence_River&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Modern Canada")]

[Alcoa](/wiki/Alcoa "Alcoa"), [Reynolds Metals Company](/wiki/Reynolds_Metals_Company "Reynolds Metals Company"), and [General Motors (GM) Central Foundry](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") operated along the St. Lawrence River and its tributaries for decades. The Alcoa plant opened in 1903, and Reynolds and GM began operations in the late 1950s. These facilities released toxic substances into the St. Lawrence River and the surrounding area, including [PCBs](/wiki/PCBs "PCBs"), [PAHs](/wiki/PAHs "PAHs"), [cyanide](/wiki/Cyanide "Cyanide"), [fluoride](/wiki/Fluoride "Fluoride"), and [dioxins](/wiki/Polychlorinated_dibenzodioxins "Polychlorinated dibenzodioxins").

During the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the [Battle of the St. Lawrence](/wiki/Battle_of_the_St._Lawrence "Battle of the St. Lawrence") involved submarine and anti-submarine actions throughout the lower St. Lawrence River and the entire [Gulf of St. Lawrence](/wiki/Gulf_of_St._Lawrence "Gulf of St. Lawrence"), [Strait of Belle Isle](/wiki/Strait_of_Belle_Isle "Strait of Belle Isle") and [Cabot Strait](/wiki/Cabot_Strait "Cabot Strait") from May to October 1942, September 1943, and again in October and November 1944. During this time, German [U-boats](/wiki/U-boat "U-boat") sank several merchant marine ships and three Canadian warships.

In the late 1970s, the river was the subject of a successful ecological campaign (called "Save the River"), originally responding to planned development by the [United States Army Corps of Engineers](/wiki/United_States_Army_Corps_of_Engineers "United States Army Corps of Engineers"). The campaign was organized, among others, by [Abbie Hoffman](/wiki/Abbie_Hoffman "Abbie Hoffman").[[50]](#cite_note-50)

* The novel and film *[Black Robe](/wiki/Black_Robe "Black Robe")* are set primarily on the St. Lawrence River during the 17th century.
* The 1941 children's book *[Paddle-to-the-Sea](/wiki/Paddle-to-the-Sea "Paddle-to-the-Sea")*, and the film *[Paddle to the Sea](/wiki/Paddle_to_the_Sea "Paddle to the Sea")*, involve passage through the St. Lawrence River.
* The St. Lawrence River is mentioned in the 1967 single [Canadian Railroad Trilogy](/wiki/Canadian_Railroad_Trilogy "Canadian Railroad Trilogy") by [Gordon Lightfoot](/wiki/Gordon_Lightfoot "Gordon Lightfoot").
